name: market gravestone
shortDescription: A market-ready gravestone kept in stock for practical mourners, village yards, and buyers who cannot wait for custom carving. It is plain enough to move quickly, solid enough to matter, and priced for grief that arrives without appointment.
flavorText: “Even sorrow has urgent orders.”

name: large gravestone
shortDescription: A large gravestone with enough size for a proper inscription, a family mark, and the dignity of weight. It costs more to haul than to admire, but buyers who choose it usually want memory to stand visibly above the grass.
flavorText: “Heavy stone is how remembrance refuses to whisper.”

name: wooded grave cross
shortDescription: A wooden grave cross shaped for modest burials, roadside shrines, and cemeteries where carved stone is too dear or too slow. It weathers faster than marble, but its plain form carries a gentler kind of respect.
flavorText: “Wood remembers softly, then returns to the earth.”

name: defend
shortDescription: A symbolic defend token used in lesson boards, spell diagrams, tactical games, and guild exercises where protection must be shown as clearly as movement. Teachers like it because the idea is simple: hold the line before counting the profit.
flavorText: “The shield drawn on paper still teaches the hand where to stand.”

name: alpha
shortDescription: A marked alpha piece, often used as a first-rank token in magical alphabets, training sets, coded ledgers, and academic games. Its value is modest, but scholars and tutors treat beginnings with unreasonable seriousness.
flavorText: “Every system wants to pretend its first sign explains the rest.”

name: beta
shortDescription: A beta-marked study token, useful in counting lessons, coded orders, and spellwork diagrams that require a second step before anything becomes interesting. It rarely stands alone, but sets and classrooms become incomplete without it.
flavorText: “Second marks are where confidence begins to need structure.”

name: delta
shortDescription: A delta token cut or painted for scholars, navigators, alchemists, and anyone tracking change from one state to another. It is bought in small sets by people who like their mysteries arranged in symbols before being misunderstood.
flavorText: “Delta is the mark a thing wears when it refuses to stay still.”

name: epsilon
shortDescription: An epsilon token from a learned symbol set, light in the hand but heavy with academic pretension. It appears in puzzle boxes, spell primers, and lecture halls where naming a thing makes everyone feel closer to controlling it.
flavorText: “Small signs become large when a teacher points at them.”

name: eta
shortDescription: An eta-marked piece used in formal alphabets, warding exercises, and games of ordered memory. It has little worth by itself, yet collectors pay attention because missing middle symbols make even expensive sets look careless.
flavorText: “The middle of a sequence is where lost pieces hide.”

name: gamma
shortDescription: A gamma token with a scholar's sharp little mark, traded among tutors, enchanters, and puzzle makers who need recognizable signs in durable form. It is not rare, but good carving keeps chalk dust out of serious work.
flavorText: “Gamma looks important because students are told it is.”

name: iota
shortDescription: A tiny iota token, small enough to be misplaced and important enough that someone will complain when it is. It is popular in grammar sets, coded counters, and magical notation where even the smallest mark can change the reading.
flavorText: “Never trust a small sign to stay harmless.”

name: theta
shortDescription: A theta-marked token used by mathematicians, star readers, and spell tutors who enjoy symbols that look like secrets. It has the pleasing shape of a sealed eye, which makes buyers assign it more mystery than the maker probably intended.
flavorText: “A circle with a line is enough to start three arguments.”

name: zeta
shortDescription: A zeta token from an ordered set of teaching marks, often found in academy kits, puzzle boards, and tidy spell tables. It is usually bought with its companions, because lone letters are hard to sell unless a collector is desperate.
flavorText: “Every alphabet has a sign that waits quietly for its turn.”

name: person
shortDescription: A person symbol token used for maps, exercises, social ledgers, and games where a living actor must be represented without naming anyone. It is simple, useful, and strangely powerful once placed beside arrows, coins, or danger marks.
flavorText: “A single figure on a board can make the whole plan feel personal.”

name: movement
shortDescription: A movement marker showing travel, action, or a change of position in lessons, maps, and magical diagrams. Caravans use such signs for routes, instructors use them for drills, and puzzle makers use them to make simple things difficult.
flavorText: “A mark for movement is still until the mind follows it.”

name: heal
shortDescription: A heal token used in temple lessons, alchemy charts, field ledgers, and board games where recovery must be counted like any other resource. Its gentle mark makes it popular with apprentices who prefer mending to memorizing wound lists.
flavorText: “Healing looks simple only when reduced to a symbol.”

name: receive
shortDescription: A receive marker used in trade games, teaching ledgers, and ritual diagrams to show goods, messages, favors, or obligations coming into hand. Merchants understand it quickly, because every business is built around what arrives and what is still promised.
flavorText: “Receiving is just taking with paperwork nearby.”

name: trade
shortDescription: A trade symbol token for market lessons, guild boards, classroom ledgers, and negotiation games. It is often placed between two sides to show exchange, though any merchant knows the true bargain usually hides in what the symbol leaves out.
flavorText: “Two arrows make trade look fairer than it feels.”

name: consume
shortDescription: A consume marker used to show use, eating, burning, spending, or any other act that turns stock into memory. Cooks, alchemists, quartermasters, and teachers all need it, because every system eventually asks where the goods went.
flavorText: “Consumption is the quiet end of inventory.”

name: justice
shortDescription: A justice token carried in law lessons, temple sets, civic games, and judgment diagrams where fairness must be represented by something small enough to move around a board. Its buyers often disagree loudly about what it means.
flavorText: “Justice is easier to carve than to deliver.”

name: learn
shortDescription: A learn marker used by tutors, guild trainers, and magical schools to show study, progress, or a lesson still being absorbed. It is a humble token, but it appears in expensive rooms because knowledge likes to dress itself in tools.
flavorText: “Learning begins when the student stops admiring the diagram.”

name: bless
shortDescription: A bless token marked for temple boards, charm sets, and ritual teaching where approval from above must be shown without inviting theological debate. Priests value it for instruction, while merchants value it because blessed-looking pieces sell briskly near holy days.
flavorText: “A small blessing travels better than a long sermon.”

name: disasters
shortDescription: A disasters marker used in planning boards, prophecy games, caravan lessons, and cautionary charts. It represents floods, fires, raids, failed harvests, and all the other events that make merchants wish they had bought insurance instead of optimism.
flavorText: “One little token, enough bad news for a season.”

name: powers
shortDescription: A powers token used to represent many forces at once: noble privilege, magic, engines, spirits, or the invisible pressures that move a kingdom. Its meaning depends on the board, which is why scholars buy labels and merchants buy duplicates.
flavorText: “Plural power is how teachers avoid choosing a side.”

name: elements
shortDescription: An elements marker used in alchemy, weather study, magical drills, and teaching sets where earth, air, fire, water, and less obedient forces must share one sign. It is small, but it suggests the whole workshop may soon become complicated.
flavorText: “The elements fit neatly on a token and nowhere else.”

name: apocalypse
shortDescription: An apocalypse marker for dramatic prophecy boards, end-time sermons, historical games, and scholars who enjoy naming the worst possible outcome. It is rarely needed in practical trade, though caravan masters use similar marks when routes become truly hopeless.
flavorText: “A useful symbol for when all other plans have become comedy.”

name: power
shortDescription: A power token showing force, authority, magic, or raw capacity depending on who owns the board. It is deliberately broad, which makes it useful in classrooms and suspicious in negotiations where everyone claims to know what power means.
flavorText: “Power is always clearer when someone else is holding the token.”

name: leap
shortDescription: A leap marker used for movement lessons, agility games, spell diagrams, and stories where a figure must cross more space than ordinary steps allow. It is popular with young students because jumping always feels more heroic than walking.
flavorText: “A leap is just a decision that leaves the floor.”

name: attack
shortDescription: An attack token used in tactical boards, training games, and martial lessons where pressure must be represented clearly before anyone argues about bravery. It is a simple mark for a direct act, though commanders know direct acts are rarely simple.
flavorText: “On a board, attack is clean. Off the board, it gets expensive.”

name: knowledge
shortDescription: A knowledge token used in libraries, academy games, spell schools, and guild tests where understanding must be counted like a resource. It is often placed beside locked doors or hidden paths, because stories love pretending wisdom arrives on schedule.
flavorText: “Knowledge is the only cargo that gets heavier when shared.”

name: shine
shortDescription: A shine marker used for light, polish, fame, magical glow, or anything made more valuable by being noticed. Jewelers, enchanters, and teachers all use the sign differently, but every merchant understands the price of catching the eye.
flavorText: “Shine is what value does when it wants witnesses.”

name: courage
shortDescription: A courage token for virtue lessons, heroic games, temple instruction, and morale boards where fear must be answered with something visible. Its buyers tend to speak grandly, but even a small symbol can help a young recruit remember the point.
flavorText: “Courage is easier to carry before the road tests it.”

name: drink
shortDescription: A drink marker used in tavern games, ration ledgers, cooking lessons, and healing charts to show a consumed liquid without naming the cup. It is especially useful when the difference between water, wine, tonic, and poison matters later.
flavorText: “A drink token never admits what was poured.”

name: danger
shortDescription: A danger marker for maps, training boards, warning charts, and games where trouble must be placed before someone walks into it. Scouts buy it for clarity, teachers buy it for drama, and merchants buy it because danger sells lessons quickly.
flavorText: “Danger is the cheapest thing to mark and the costliest to ignore.”

name: master
shortDescription: A master token used to mark command, expertise, ownership of a lesson, or the final rank in a training set. Apprentices often resent it, teachers often enjoy it, and merchants know any title piece raises the price of the whole set.
flavorText: “Mastery is a word people carve before they prove it.”

name: experience
shortDescription: An experience marker for training games, guild tests, story ledgers, and lessons where practice must be shown as progress. It represents the invisible value of having failed before, which is why old traders respect it more than students do.
flavorText: “Experience is what remains after the mistake stops being new.”

name: sacrifice
shortDescription: A sacrifice token used in temple lessons, strategy boards, heroic tales, and hard accounting where giving something up must be made visible. Its meaning changes by context, but it always asks the same merchant question: what was paid, and why?
flavorText: “Sacrifice is loss with a reason attached.”

name: own
shortDescription: An own marker used in property lessons, trade boards, and legal demonstrations to show possession without drawing a whole warehouse around it. It is a simple sign, but simple signs start most arguments about land, tools, beasts, and debts.
flavorText: “Ownership begins as a mark and ends as paperwork.”

name: health
shortDescription: A health token used by healers, trainers, game masters, and quartermasters who need a quick sign for condition and recovery. It is bought in sets because health is easiest to count before anyone actually needs it restored.
flavorText: “Health is the number everyone notices once it falls.”

name: love
shortDescription: A love token for festival games, temple lessons, fortune boards, and dramatic storytellers who need affection made portable. It is sentimental stock, but sells reliably near weddings, pilgrimages, and any market where loneliness has spending money.
flavorText: “Love fits on a token only because real hearts refuse inventory.”

name: action
shortDescription: An action marker used to show that a figure, player, student, or spell is doing something instead of merely waiting. It is one of the most useful tokens in any teaching set, because plans become clearer once motion receives a price.
flavorText: “Action is the point where intention starts leaving marks.”

name: heritage
shortDescription: A heritage token used in noble lessons, family records, cultural games, and temple displays where ancestry must be represented without unrolling an entire genealogy. Buyers treat it carefully, because inherited meaning is never as light as the piece itself.
flavorText: “Heritage is memory with witnesses.”

name: resistance
shortDescription: A resistance marker for civic games, virtue lessons, military drills, and magical diagrams where pressure is met without surrender. It sells well in troubled regions, though merchants wisely avoid asking whether buyers mean politics, weather, poison, or pride.
flavorText: “Resistance is the art of not moving when pushed.”

name: skill
shortDescription: A skill token used by guilds, tutors, trainers, and game boards to mark practiced ability rather than raw luck. Apprentices like earning it, masters like withholding it, and merchants like selling whole sets to both sides.
flavorText: “Skill is talent that agreed to be corrected.”

name: stealth
shortDescription: A stealth marker used in scouting lessons, shadow games, strategy boards, and stories where remaining unseen matters more than standing tall. It is small by design, often darkly marked, and frequently the first token lost under a table.
flavorText: “The best stealth token is the one everyone accuses you of misplacing.”

name: words
shortDescription: A words token for rhetoric lessons, spellcraft, diplomacy games, and ledgers where speech itself becomes the important tool. Scribes and tutors like it because it reminds students that a phrase can open doors, close deals, or ruin breakfast.
flavorText: “Words are the lightest goods and the hardest to take back.”

name: wealth
shortDescription: A wealth marker used in trade games, noble ledgers, inheritance lessons, and moral tales where coin must be represented without spilling actual coin. It is popular because people enjoy moving wealth around safely, even when the board is imaginary.
flavorText: “Wealth on a token behaves better than wealth in a purse.”

name: give
shortDescription: A give marker used in teaching ledgers, ritual diagrams, charity games, and trade exercises where something moves from one hand to another. It looks generous, though merchants know giving often returns later wearing a different hat.
flavorText: “Giving is trade with softer language.”

name: wooden bowl
shortDescription: A simple wooden bowl for porridge, stew, nuts, herbs, dye work, or any household task that does not require fine ceramic manners. It is light, durable, and common enough that a caravan cook can lose three before anyone panics.
flavorText: “A wooden bowl is humble until supper arrives.”

name: wooden plate
shortDescription: A flat wooden plate suited to taverns, camps, farm kitchens, and households where breakable tableware is an unnecessary argument. It stains, dents, and survives, which makes it more useful than many finer things on a rough road.
flavorText: “The best travel plate is one that forgives being dropped.”
